title: Add refunds endpoint and customer transaction history
summary: The panel's strongest and correct signal is the PCI exposure: c25 surfaces a full card_number being placed into the history response and stays a `must`. Everything else that was marked `must` is real but not blocking-by-the-bar (no secret/RCE/auth-bypass), so I downgraded them to `should`: the module-level refundsStore singleton (c0), the two perf hot-path issues (c9, c10), the 200-instead-of-404 contract bug (c13), and the weak test assertions (c14). I merged the considerable redundancy: all six console.log findings across both files collapse into c17; the duplicate amount-capping correctness finding folds into c1; the 200-vs-404 pair (c7→c13); the three weak-assertion test findings (c15,c16→c14); the type-cast red flag onto the PCI finding since it's the same line (c6→c25); the snake_case/camelCase response complaints (c22→c21); and the NxM scan described at both controller and service into c10 (c12). Kept the real correctness catch in c20 (undefined sort key + `==`), the KISS refactors, the unused schema (c8), and the schema-validation tightening (c23). No outright false positives worth dropping.

findings.title: Module-level singleton refundsStore will leak state across tests
findings.message: The refundsStore is a hidden global Record shared across all tests and all app instances. This violates the injection pattern used everywhere else (see TransactionRepository). Tests will appear to pass but will fail mysteriously if test order changes or when run in parallel. Either create a RefundsRepository interface and inject it like TransactionRepository, or pass the store into RefundsService as a dependency. The current pattern is a test-pollution landmine.

findings.title: Silent refund amount conversion and capping hides bugs
findings.message: The refund logic silently converts dollars to cents and caps to the transaction amount if the client sends too much. This masks a client error: if a client sends the wrong unit or amount, they get silently capped instead of rejected. This will cause refund mismatches in logs and customer complaints. Either validate strictly (throw if refund > transaction) or explicitly allow partial refunds with clear semantics. Don't cap silently.

findings.title: Manual loop should use .filter() and .map()
findings.message: Iterating with a for loop and an undefined guard, then pushing to an array, is three lines of ceremony for what `.filter()` does in one: `return Object.values(refundsStore).filter(r => r.transactionId === transactionId);` Clearer intent, no need to initialize `out`, no undefined guard.

findings.title: Manual accumulation loop should use .reduce()
findings.message: Instead of: `for (const r of refunds) { total = total + r.amount; }` use: `const total = refunds.reduce((sum, r) => sum + r.amount, 0);` Same logic, one line, clearer intent.

findings.title: Manual filter loop should use .filter()
findings.message: The loop `for (let i = 0; i < all.length; i++)` with an undefined guard and push to `mine` is `.filter(tx => tx && tx.customerId == customerId)` but longer. Use the built-in method. Also note: when would `tx` be undefined coming from `.store.values()`? The guard is unnecessary.

findings.title: Manual sort comparator is verbose; use date comparison
findings.message: The three-way comparison (if/if/return) is correct but verbose. Since the field is ISO string (`created_at`), you can compare directly: `mine.sort((a, b) => b.created_at.localeCompare(a.created_at))` or via Date: `.sort((a, b) => new Date(b.created_at).getTime() - new Date(a.created_at).getTime())`. Clearer intent, fewer lines.

findings.title: Unused validator schema
findings.message: `historyQuerySchema` is defined but never imported or used. Delete it (YAGNI). If you need it later, git has the history.

findings.title: Load-all-then-filter-in-memory on hot path
findings.message: The endpoint fetches all transactions from the repository and filters in JavaScript instead of pushing the predicate to the store. `const all = await this.transactions.findAll()` loads the entire table into memory. This is O(all transactions) per request and will OOM or timeout once you have thousands of transactions in the database. Even at 50k transactions, you're pulling all 50k into memory on every customer history request to find maybe 100 matches. Fix: Add a `findByCustomerId(customerId)` method to TransactionRepository that returns only matching transactions using an indexed WHERE clause.

findings.title: N×M query pattern: enrich loop calls scan-based lookup
findings.message: For each customer transaction, the code calls `enrichTransactionWithRefunds(tx)`, which in turn calls `listRefundsForTransaction()` (refundsService.ts:59–68). That method scans the entire refundsStore to find matches. Cost is O(customer transactions × total refunds). Example: 100 customer transactions × 10,000 total refunds in the store = 1,000,000 iterations per request. Fix: Batch-load all refunds at the start of the endpoint using a `findByTransactionIds(txIds)` method, then join in memory. Also add an index on refundsStore by transactionId to make future single-lookups O(1).

findings.title: Missing pagination on unbounded list endpoint
findings.message: GET /customers/:id/transactions returns all matching transactions with no limit or pagination. A customer with 10,000 transactions returns all 10,000. Combined with the load-all and N×M problems above, this is a memory and latency bomb. Fix: Add query parameters (`limit` and `cursor` or `offset`) to page results. Cap to a reasonable size like 50 per page. Implement cursor-based pagination if you expect large result sets.

findings.title: Missing transaction returns 200 instead of 404
findings.message: When a transaction doesn't exist, the endpoint returns `200 OK` with an empty JSON body. This is dishonest HTTP semantics — a missing resource should return `404 Not Found`. A client calling this endpoint cannot tell success from not-found; all they see is a successful request. Return `res.status(404).json({ error: 'transaction not found' })` instead.

findings.title: Test assertion passes regardless of correctness
findings.message: `expect(refund.body).toBeDefined()` will pass whether the refund succeeded, failed, or returned garbage. Assert the actual contract: `expect(refund.status).toBe(201)` and `expect(refund.body).toMatchObject({ refundId: expect.any(String), transactionId: charge.body.id, amount: 1000 })`.

findings.title: console.log used instead of structured logger
findings.message: The codebase imports `pinoHttp` and should use the injected logger. `console.log` bypasses structured logging, log levels, and the pino redaction allowlist. Use `this.logger.info({ customerId }, 'history requested')` or accept a logger in the constructor.

findings.title: Comparison operator and naming convention drift
findings.message: Line 21 uses `tx.customerId == customerId` — should be `===`. Also, the sort on line 27-32 tries to access `created_at` (snake_case) but the Transaction model uses `createdAt` (camelCase), so the sort key is undefined for every row, making the sort a no-op that looks like it works. Use `a.createdAt` instead.

findings.title: Naming convention inconsistency in response
findings.message: The response mixes snake_case (`card_number`, `customer_id`, `gateway_reference`, `created_at`, `refund_total`) with the domain model's camelCase. Pick one: if the API contract is camelCase, transform in the response; if it's snake_case, be consistent. Line 40 also casts to access `card_number` which doesn't exist on Transaction — should use `tx.cardLast4` directly.

findings.title: Weak validation on refund amount
findings.message: `amount: z.number().optional()` accepts zero, negative, and fractional amounts. For a payment amount measured in cents, use `z.number().int().positive().optional()` to reject nonsensical inputs at the boundary. The ambiguous handling of dollars vs cents in refundsService (lines 36-43) compounds the issue — validate at the edge so business logic can trust its inputs.

findings.title: API response includes full card number field, violates PCI-DSS
findings.message: Line 40 attempts to include `card_number` in the transaction response: `card_number: (tx as unknown as { card_number?: string }).card_number ?? tx.cardLast4`. This exposes the full PAN if the Transaction object ever contains it, and even if it doesn't currently, the code structure signals intent to leak card data. PCI-DSS strictly forbids returning plaintext PAN in API responses. Remove the `card_number` field entirely — only return `card_last4` for legitimate business needs. If full card data is stored in Transaction, refactor to never serialize it in HTTP responses.
